# Transcrição: 03-Now What_ Analyzing and Acting on Your Data

## [00:00 - 03:02]

Once you've collected your data, the real fun begins as you analyze that information and figure out the best course of action. Let's start with trends. Trends are like the North Star guiding your business strategy. They reveal the direction in which your market is moving. For example, consider a boutique owner who notices a growing trend of customers seeking sustainable and ethically grown products. By analyzing sales data and customer feedback, the owner can identify the demand for eco-friendly products and adjust their inventory to attract more customers. You can ask Gigi to help you identify trends in your data. Next, let's talk about patterns. Patterns are the hidden gems within your data that provide valuable insights into consumer behavior. They help you understand how different factors influence each other and can uncover new opportunities. For instance, imagine a restaurant owner is analyzing customer preferences and discovers a pattern, more demand for plant-based menu items on the weekends. Armed with this information, the owner decides to introduce a weekend special featuring plant-based dishes, attracting more customers and increasing revenue. Check out your worksheet to see how Gigi can help you identify patterns in your data. In today's FAS-based business world, knowing what your competitors are up to is crucial. It helps you identify gaps in the market, anticipate industry trends and stay one step ahead of the competition. So how can you use data to gain a competitive advantage? Let me introduce you to the three pillars of competitive analysis. Collect, analyze and act. Just like you did for yourself, you also must collect data on your competitors. This includes everything from product offerings and pricing strategies to their marketing tactics and customer reviews. For example, imagine you're starting a health and wellness coaching business. By collecting data from larger companies like Noom about their offerings, pricing and customer reviews, you can find ways to make your business stand out, such as offering individualized menus, creating support communities, or providing simple health education. Next, analyze the data you've collected to uncover insights. Look for patterns, trends and outliers that can help you understand your competitor's strengths and weaknesses. For instance, if you're an online personal stylist analyzing data on your competitor's sales and customer demographics, you might discover that there is a growing demand for sustainable fashion among younger consumers. This insight would lead you to source more sustainable fabrics and promote your eco-friendly practices to attract this segment of the market. Finally, act on the insights that you've gained from your analysis. Use the data to inform your business decisions and create strategies to stay ahead of competitors. Say your financial planning service, analyzing data on your competitor's product features

## [03:02 - 05:44]

and customer feedback. You might decide to develop a more user-friendly interface or offer better customer support to differentiate yourself in the market. It's important to know how to harness the power of marketing data to create impactful content, design engaging courses and focus your coaching efforts like never before. Let's start with content. Imagine you're a health coach looking to create content that resonates with your audience. By analyzing data on your client's interests and pain points, you can tailor your content to address their specific needs. For example, if you discover that your audience is interested in plant-based diets, you might create a series of recipes and meal plans around that lifestyle, demonstrating your expertise and building trust with your audience. Another example could be educating your audience with videos or blog posts that address common health misconceptions backed by scientific research. Next, let's talk about course development. As an online course creator, understanding your target audience's learning styles and challenges is key. By analyzing course completion rates, engagement levels and feedback, you can identify areas where your course can be improved. For instance, let's say that your students aren't as engaged in your text-based course. Through interviews and social listening, you realize that your audience of ultramarathoners would love an audio-only course that they can listen to on their long runs. This insight could enable you to create a unique program that meets their wants and needs where others aren't. And finally, let's discuss coaching focus. Whether you are a life coach, business coach or any other type of coach, market research can help you tailor your coaching content to meet your client's needs. For example, your career coach analyzing data on your target audience, Gen Z job applicants in the tech field. You notice that more and more of the people in the group want to improve their social and communication skills. Something that you already offer, but haven't highlighted. By adding that outcome prominently in your marketing material, you can connect with them more directly. To leverage these insights effectively, it's crucial to adopt a structured approach. Start by identifying the trends, patterns and outliers in both your data and your competitors. Then analyze that data to understand the implications of your business as you create, refine and market your offer to your ideal clients. Remember, research is not just about looking for facts. It's the key to unlocking new opportunities and staying ahead of the competition. It's how you can connect with your audience on a deeper level and achieve greater impact. Now that you know the steps, it's time to embrace the power of market research in your business.
